title: Planned Feature: Employee Tracking + Leaderboard
content:
Hi Climbers,

we are planning a new feature around employee tracking, and before building it we want to collect your feedback in detail, because we think this could become one of the most powerful ways to help local businesses generate more reviews in a natural and consistent way.

The idea is this.

The business owner will have a dedicated section called Employees Links, where they can add the employees who are responsible for asking customers to leave reviews. Adding an employee will be very simple: the business just inserts a photo, a name, and an alias, which can also simply be the employee’s real name or nickname.

Every time an employee is added, the platform will automatically generate a dedicated review link for that employee. It will also be possible to download the related QR code. Technically, this employee link will simply be the general review link with an extra URL parameter, something like employee=alias.

In this way, we will be able to track the unique visits to each employee’s review link. So if an employee asks a customer to scan their own QR code or open their own review link, the business will be able to measure how many unique visits that employee has generated.

On top of this, we want to build a leaderboard, inspired by the Skool style, where the business can see the ranking of employees based on the number of unique visits to each employee’s review link. The leaderboard will be viewable for the last 7 days, the last 30 days, or all-time.

This would already create an interesting use case for businesses. For example, the company could decide that, on the last day of every month, the employee with the highest number of unique visits receives a bonus in payroll, because that employee made a stronger effort to ask customers for reviews.

But we want to take this feature much further.

Our idea is that if, inside the text of the review, the customer mentions the alias of the employee, then that review will be automatically assigned to that employee. For example, if the review says “Luke was very kind with us”, the review will automatically be associated with the employee whose alias is “Luke”.

This means that inside the leaderboard, in addition to the ranking by unique visits, there could also be a ranking by number of reviews assigned to each employee.

In this way, employees would not only be encouraged to ask customers for a review, but also to invite them to write the review mentioning their name or nickname. For example, they could say something like: “If you leave a review, mention my name, Luke, because it helps me improve and grow inside the company.”

We believe this can work very well because end customers are often more willing to leave a review for a person than for a company, especially when there has been a real human relationship, a friendly conversation, a positive interaction, or a good service moment. In many cases, it is easier for a customer to write about the employee as a person than to write a generic review about the business.

Because of this, the leaderboard could be ordered either by number of unique visits or by number of attributed reviews, and the business could decide its own internal bonus rules. For example, it could say that the employee with the highest number of unique visits every month receives a $100 bonus in payroll, while every review that mentions the employee’s alias is worth an additional $20.

This would naturally create a healthy competition between employees, both on who generates more visits to their review link and on who gets more reviews mentioning their name.

From a product point of view, we also want the leaderboard to have a very nice and gamified UI. Another important point is that the leaderboard could be shared via a public external link with the whole team, without requiring every employee to have a user account inside the platform. In this way, all employees could monitor the ranking, check their position, and track how many reviews they have won, without needing access to the software itself.

There is also an important branding benefit for the business.

Reviews that talk about the staff, and even mention their names, feel much more authentic and real than reviews that talk only in general terms about the company. At the end of the day, we are all people, and our relationships are based on interpersonal connections. Having reviews that talk not only about the quality of the service or product, but also about the people working in the business, helps the company communicate a healthier, more trustworthy, and more human image to potential customers who may have never visited the business before.

So this is the feature we are thinking about.

We would love to know what you think. Do you believe your clients would use it? Do you think employees would respond well to this kind of incentive? Is there anything you would add, remove, or improve?

This feature will be developed after the GEO Agent, so most likely in the second half of April.

Let us know your feedback in the comments.

I slept on it [@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) and gained some new insights.

Like I said, I'm very excited and thought this feature \(by the roadmap info\) it would be a simple leaderboard for link/QR code for the team members but you and your team want to make that extra mile! BETTER, but it also creates other chances and challenges.

What I like is that it makes asking for reviews more personal and more measurable. In a lot of cases, customers remember the employee who helped them more than the business name itself. So if reviews mention real staff members, the reviews will feel more authentic and human.

I also like that every employee gets their own link \(for NFC use\) and QR code. That makes it easier for businesses to track who is really making the effort.

A few things I would improve.

First, I would not make it only about a leaderboard. A leaderboard is good, but usually it mainly motivates the people already at the top. I think it would be stronger if you also add things like goals, badges, streaks, team targets \(some businesses have different departments\), or maybe a most improved section.

Second, I think businesses should be able to choose how they want to rank employees. Some will care about unique visits, others about attributed reviews, others about conversion rate from visits to reviews. That flexibility would make it much stronger.

Third, I think trust in the numbers is very important. If employees can game the system by scanning their own link/QR code or creating fake visits, the value of the leaderboard drops very fast. So I would definitely add protection against abuse and make it clear how visits are counted.

I also like the idea of assigning reviews when the customer mentions the employee’s name or alias, but I would not rely only on exact matching. People misspell names, use nicknames, or mention more than one person. So if possible, I would improve that logic with nickname matching, fuzzy matching, or manual confirmation when needed.

Another thing I would add is a recognition layer. Not only tracking, but also something like employee of the month \(e.g. Mc Donalds\), reward tracking, or something simple businesses can use internally to motivate the team.

And for the public leaderboard, I would make that optional. In some businesses it will work very well, but in others it can create pressure or resistance. So giving different visibility options would be smart.

So yes, I'm excited and know for sure my \(new\) clients as well, but for me the key is this: make it easy to use, hard to game, and flexible enough for different types of businesses.

Hope this helps.

title: 🧗 Welcome To The Climbo Accelerator — Start Here
content:
Welcome to the Climbo Accelerator!

This space is built for marketing agency owners who want to add a real, long-term SaaS business line to their agency using Climbo. The goal is simple: help you generate predictable monthly recurring revenue while delivering concrete, measurable results to your local business clients.

The Classroom is your structured learning hub. To get started, head to 👉 [https://www.skool.com/climbo/classroom](https://www.skool.com/climbo/classroom) and begin with the “Start Here” course. This course explains what Climbo is, how the platform works, and—most importantly—how your agency can use it to create new MRR by upselling it to your existing local business clients. It will give you the strategic foundation you need before touching the product or speaking to clients.

Once you’ve completed the Start Here course, activate your Climbo account and move on to the “5-Day Implementation Plan” course. This course is entirely practical. It walks you step by step through setting up your Climbo account correctly during the first 5 days—white-labeling the platform, connecting Stripe, creating your pricing plans, and testing the full client onboarding flow—so you can start selling immediately and see real results from the platform as quickly as possible.

Inside the Community, everything is organized into a few clear areas. Updates is where we share product announcements, feature releases, new resources, and important news about Climbo. Wins is the space where members share progress, milestones, and results—whether it’s onboarding a first client or hitting a new MRR goal. Gems is dedicated to high-value insights, strategies, tools, and lessons learned that can help others move faster. Support is for questions that may be useful to the community as a whole, while account-specific issues should always be handled through the support ticket form inside the Climbo platform. Intros is where you introduce yourself, share your background, and explain your goals, helping create real connections and collaboration. Feel free to introduce yourself in the Intros category — tell us who you are, what kind of agency you run, and what you’re aiming to build.

Alongside this, the community Calendar is a key part of the experience. We host weekly live sessions every Thursday at 4:00 PM \(Rome Time\) focused on planning, product updates, feedback, and open Q&A. These sessions give you direct access to the Climbo team and help you stay aligned with what we’re building and how to use it effectively.

We’re glad you’re here.

Giacomo, Tommaso, and the Climbo Team 🧑‍💻💚

title: 📝 GEO Agent Is Now Live + AMA Replay (16/04/2026)
content:
Hi Climbers!

the GEO Agent will be released today in a few hours.

This is one of the most important features we’ve released so far. It allows you to help your clients automatically generate localized, SEO-focused content with the goal of improving their visibility and ranking on Google.

The GEO Agent takes real business data — services, categories, and especially customer reviews — and turns it into relevant blog articles and content ideas designed to support local SEO over time.

This means your clients are not only collecting reviews, but also using them to build content that strengthens their online presence.

If you want to see exactly how it works, we highly recommend watching the AMA replay from yesterday, where we did a full overview of the feature and how to use it in your offer.

By default, the GEO Agent is disabled in your plans, so you’ll need to activate it manually for the clients you want to give access to it.

This is just the first version, and we’ll keep improving it based on your feedback.

Let us know your thoughts once you start using it.

We’ve also released a new UI for the Plans section.

It’s now much easier to create and manage your custom plans. Instead of a long list of features, everything is now organized into 5 clear areas: Details, Reputation, Visibility, Engagement, Price.

This is purely a UI improvement, so you don’t need to change anything in your existing plans.

Climbo Team 👨🏻‍💻🫡

comments.content: Thanks for the developments, enjoying the proactivity

This is really good! One question though: could we consider offering an option to embed the blog on a website instead of using a subdomain? The reasoning is that blog.mywebsite.com is technically a standalone website distinct from mywebsite.com and this could dilute authority. Furthermore, the main website has elements like a header and footer and linking to a subdomain would alter the design. If we allowed embedding the blog directly into the website, we’d eliminate this problem. What are your thoughts?

replies.content: [@Matteo Bero](obj://user/738b772c302d4a85ba7761424cc3dd2b) Normally, I think that if you specify a subdomain of your main domain, the SEO will be tied to it. It’s the main domain that counts, if I’m not mistaken.

[@Christophe Gx](obj://user/e5ad25faf85c479b961429bfecb20b57) I think subdomain vs subfolder is quite a nuanced SEO topic. A subdomain is technically treated as a separate site, although Google can associate it with the main domain in some cases — but not always.

There’s a lot of debate around this, and while I think that blog in subfolders \([mywebsite.com/blog](http://mywebsite.com/blog)\) gives you the best chance to rank, I’m not trying to take a strict SEO position here.

My main concern for this GEO feature is more about overall integration. From a UX and site structure perspective, having the blog under something like [mywebsite.com/blog](http://mywebsite.com/blog) keeps everything more unified — shared navigation, internal linking, and a more consistent experience.

title: Important Update: Google Review Policy Compliance
content:
Hey Climbers,

Over the last few days, there has been a lot of discussion around Google’s review policies, especially around review gating, employee mentions, incentives, QR codes, and how review requests should be handled.

We have reviewed the policy carefully, and we want to make sure Climbo continues to help businesses collect genuine customer feedback in the safest and most compliant way possible.

Google’s policy is very clear on a few key points:

“Contributions to Google Maps should reflect a genuine experience at a place or business.”

Google also states that merchants must not:

“Discourage or prohibit negative reviews, or selectively solicit positive reviews from customers.”

And when soliciting reviews, merchants should not:

“Require or pressure users to leave ratings or write reviews while on the premises, nor should they request that specific content be included.”

This includes:

“Merchants requesting that staff solicit reviews that include specific content, including content that identifies a staff member.”

At the same time, Google also clearly says that merchants are allowed to:

“Solicit or encourage the posting of content that does represent a genuine experience, without offering incentives to do so or attempting to influence the rating or the contents of the review.”

So the goal is not to stop collecting reviews.

The goal is to collect reviews in the right way: neutral, genuine, non-selective, and without incentives or pressure.

Here is what we are changing.

1. Review Gating: Removing Sentiment-Based Filters

We are removing review filtering based on sentiment.

This means we will no longer support flows where the customer is first asked to choose a rating, stars, thumbs up/down, smiley face, or any other sentiment indicator, and then receives a different experience based on that response.

For example, this type of flow is no longer considered safe:

Happy customer → sent to Google
Unhappy customer → sent to private feedback form

That can be interpreted as selectively soliciting positive reviews and discouraging negative reviews.

Instead, we are moving to a neutral two-choice approach.

Every customer will see the same two options, without any sentiment filtering:

Share Your Experience Publicly
The customer can leave a genuine public review on the available platforms.

Contact the Business Directly
The customer can privately contact the business with a question, concern, or feedback.

Why this is safer:

Every customer gets equal access to the public review option. We are not asking for a rating first. We are not changing the experience based on whether the customer is happy or unhappy. We are simply giving customers two fair and neutral choices: share publicly or contact the business directly.

This aligns much better with Google’s requirement not to “discourage or prohibit negative reviews” and not to “selectively solicit positive reviews.”

The private contact option is not a filter. It is simply a direct communication channel for customers who want to speak with the business.

2. Employee Alias Tracking: Moving Away From Staff Names

We are also updating how employee aliases work.

Google’s policy says merchants should not request that specific content be included in reviews, including content that identifies a staff member.

Because of this, we are making the employee alias feature safer.

We will block the ability to use obvious first names, last names, or parts of employee names as aliases.

We will also add a clear warning in the UI explaining that Google does not allow businesses to ask customers to mention staff members by name in reviews.

Instead of using staff names, we will encourage businesses to use safer internal labels such as:

Department name
Service name
Product name
Role
Branch
Team
Treatment/service category

Examples:

Customer Support
Hair Coloring
Dental Cleaning
Sales Team
Reception
Room Service
Milan Branch
Installation Team

The purpose of the alias should be internal tracking, not asking the customer to mention a person.

If one of these safe aliases naturally appears in the review text, the review can still be automatically assigned to the related employee, team, department, or service.

For example, if a review says:

“The hair coloring service was excellent.”

And the alias is “Hair Coloring,” the system can assign that review internally.

But the business should not ask the customer to write:

“Mention Sarah in your review.”

That is exactly the type of specific-content request Google warns against.

We are also developing manual review assignment as soon as possible. This will allow the business owner to manually assign reviews to employees after the review has been published, without needing to rely on staff names or asking customers to mention anyone.

This is the safest long-term direction: internal attribution after the review, not solicitation before the review.

3. Default Review Templates: Removing Incentives

We are updating our default review request templates.

Any default template that says something like:

“Leave us a review and enjoy 10% off your next visit”

will be removed or rewritten.

Google’s policy clearly states that merchants must not:

“Offer incentives – such as payment, discounts, free goods and/or services - in exchange for posting any review.”

So we do not want Climbo to encourage businesses to offer discounts, coupons, rewards, gifts, free products, or services in exchange for reviews.

Our default templates will now use neutral language focused on genuine customer experience.

Example:

Thank you for choosing us.
We’d love to hear about your recent experience. Your genuine feedback helps other customers learn more about our business.
You can share your experience here:
{{review_link}}

Another example:

Hi {{customer_name}}, thank you for visiting {{business_name}}.
If you’d like, you can share your genuine experience here:
{{review_link}}

The new standard is simple:

Ask for genuine feedback, not positive feedback.
Do not offer incentives.
Do not ask for 5 stars.
Do not suggest what the customer should write.
Do not ask them to mention a staff member.

4. QR Codes On-Site: Still Allowed, But Businesses Must Be Trained Properly

QR codes are not the problem.

The issue is how they are used.

Google allows merchants to encourage genuine reviews, but also says merchants should not “require or pressure users to leave ratings or write reviews while on the premises.”

So businesses can still use QR codes in-store, on cards, receipts, packaging, posters, or at the front desk.

However, staff must be trained to avoid pressure.

Safe approach:

“If you’d like, you can use this QR code later to share your genuine experience.”

Risky approach:

“Can you leave us a review right now?”

Not allowed:

“Leave us 5 stars and we’ll give you a discount.”

“Show us your review to get 10% off.”

“Please mention my name in the review.”

The right way to train staff is this:

Do not pressure customers while they are still on the premises.
Do not ask for a specific rating.
Do not offer incentives.
Do not ask for specific words or staff names.
Simply make the review option available and optional.

A QR code can remain a useful tool when it is presented as a neutral way to share a genuine experience whenever convenient.

5. Updating the Unbranded Knowledge Base

We are also updating the unbranded knowledge base.

We will remove or rewrite any content that refers to:

Filtering customer experiences through the review link
Sending only happy customers to public review platforms
Using review gating to prevent negative reviews
Offering incentives, discounts, coupons, or rewards in exchange for reviews
Asking customers to leave a specific rating
Asking customers to mention an employee or staff member
Using the employee’s real name as the recommended alias
Giving customers scripts or templates to copy into their review

The knowledge base will be repositioned around compliant review collection.

The new message will be:

Help businesses collect genuine customer feedback in a neutral, fair, and compliant way.

That means:

Same review options for every customer
No sentiment-based filtering
No incentives
No pressure
No staff-name solicitation
No suggested review content
Internal tracking only
Genuine customer experience first
Final Thoughts

We know some of these changes may feel restrictive, but they are important.

The goal is not to remove value from the platform. The goal is to protect the businesses using it.

Google still allows merchants to ask for reviews. The policy specifically says businesses may encourage content that represents a genuine experience, as long as they do not offer incentives or attempt to influence the rating or contents of the review.

That is the direction we are taking.

Climbo will continue to help businesses get more reviews, but in a safer and more sustainable way:

No review gating
No incentives
No pressure
No employee-name solicitation
No manipulation
More genuine customer feedback
Better internal tracking
Safer long-term growth

We believe this is the right move for the platform, for Climbers, and for the businesses relying on us.

Hey climbo team,

First of all, I understand why these changes are being made and I respect the intention to stay fully compliant with Google’s policies. Long term, I agree this is the safer direction.
That said, I want to share honestly how this impacts me \(and probably other partners too\).

The previous dashboard update already required a full rebuild on my side. I had just finished creating knowledge base videos, blog posts, website content, ads, flyers… basically the entire ecosystem. I accepted that, even though it meant redoing a lot of work.

Now with the removal of review filtering, I’m in a similar situation again. I’ve invested a significant amount of time and money \(around €3–4k\) into UGC content, static ads, design, blogs, SEO, and messaging. Even though filtering wasn’t my only selling point, it was still part of the positioning, so a lot of that content now needs to be reworked.
The hardest part is timing. I have clients lined up, campaigns ready to go, and now I have to pause and adjust everything again. Some prospects who were specifically interested in the filtering aspect may also drop off.

I completely understand the “why” behind these changes, but it would really help if major updates like this could involve some form of early communication, feedback round, or partner vote. Even a heads-up would allow us to prepare and avoid redoing everything last minute.

I’m still committed to the product and believe in where it’s going, just wanted to share the real impact from my side so it’s taken into account moving forward.

Thanks for listening 🙏

Be it now or in the future, the customer is still able to choose between a private and a public review, the only difference is the presentation. The bare existence of the private feedback form, even without any sentiment filter, allows you to state:
[ul][li]'Negative feedback can be privately internalised'[li]'Manage and Protect Public Image'

comments.content:
I understand where this is coming from, and I completely understand the intention behind making the platform safer and more compliant.

However, unfortunately, at least in our region, the features you are removing were the main reason why clients decided to purchase the service in the first place.

We have already sold multiple annual plans, and the functionalities being removed were exactly what convinced many businesses to join the platform.

For us, this change is honestly a major turning point. It fundamentally changes the value proposition we originally sold to our clients.

As a result, we may need to cancel several agreements, completely rethink our strategy, and seriously evaluate whether continuing with the project still makes sense for us moving forward.

I truly appreciate the transparency and the effort to adapt to Google’s policies, but I also wanted to be honest about the very significant impact these changes have on our business.

Google change is not your decision, your decision is to find a solution or let clients discover the problem too late to fix it. Obviously this is my personal opinion and a suggestion on how to proceed.

comments.content: [@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) I fully support the change. I believe compliance is of utmost importance for any business.
Without filtering, what do you see as the main selling point of the platform?

replies.content:
[@Jason Wyatt](obj://user/7c677cba0f794c3cae3cd07fa5e89f4c) The new flow still gives customers a choice at the beginning of the review link: they can either share their experience publicly or contact the business directly.

The difference is that we are no longer filtering them based on stars, thumbs up/down, or sentiment. Every customer sees the same two options, which is much safer from a compliance perspective.

In practice, many unhappy customers simply want to be heard and have their issue fixed. So when they see a clear option to contact the owner or management directly, many of them will naturally choose that path instead of posting publicly.

So the psychology is still there, but the platform is not forcing or manipulating the path. The customer chooses for themselves.

That keeps the value of the system while making it much safer for businesses long term.

[@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) I get what you're saying, I guess I just disagree. Review gating has been against terms from the beginning. Yet it has always been up to the agency owner whether or not to implement the tactic. Taking that functionality away neuters the system and makes it a much tougher sale.

[@Jason Wyatt](obj://user/7c677cba0f794c3cae3cd07fa5e89f4c) [@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf)

Jason, I have just had a chat with ChatGPT and this is what it came back with, I hope it helps:

What Google DOES allow \(and what you should lean into\)
Google explicitly allows businesses to:
[ul][li]ask customers for reviews[li]send reminders[li]use QR codes[li]automate requests[li]follow up after service[li]make review collection easier[li]encourage genuine feedback[li]increase review volume organically

That means Climbo can still aggressively market:
[ul][li]automation[li]consistency[li]timing[li]conversion optimization[li]convenience[li]response rates[li]operational workflows[li]CRM integration[li]follow-up systems[li]analytics[li]attribution[li]multi-location management[li]review velocity[li]customer engagement

The problem starts only when:
[ul][li]unhappy customers are diverted away from public reviews[li]incentives are offered[li]staff names are requested[li]specific wording is suggested[li]ratings are pressured[li]public review access changes based on sentiment

So the product still has huge value.
The positioning shift you want

Old positioning:
“Get more 5-star reviews.”
Better positioning:
“Generate more authentic customer reviews through smart automation and compliant customer engagement.”

Old:
“Filter unhappy customers.”
Better:
“Give every customer a simple way to either share publicly or contact the business directly.”

Old:
“Boost your Google rating.”
Better:
“Build a stronger online reputation through consistent review generation.”

That sounds subtle, but legally and platform-wise it’s massively different.
The real moat becomes SYSTEMIZATION

Businesses do not pay for Climbo sellers because they physically cannot ask for reviews themselves.

They pay because:
[ul][li]staff forget[li]follow-up is inconsistent[li]no automation exists[li]no analytics exist[li]no attribution exists[li]no workflows exist[li]no templates exist[li]no scaling exists[li]no centralized management exists

That is still incredibly valuable.
You can still say things like:
[ul][li]“Increase review request conversion rates”[li]“Automate customer follow-up”[li]“Generate more customer feedback”[li]“Improve review collection consistency”[li]“Make review requests frictionless”[li]“Collect more authentic reviews over time”[li]“Turn customer experiences into public social proof”

Those are all generally safe.

[@Jason Wyatt](obj://user/7c677cba0f794c3cae3cd07fa5e89f4c) highly disagree.

So many other features that help businesses. Social posting, ai agent response, blog posts and website integration to name a few.

This is a really great tool and review gating should be needed for good quality businesses.

You might just be marketing to the wrong business 🤷‍♂️

comments.content:
Hey Giacomo,

I have a suggestion for optimizing the review request process to be Google-compliant while still being business-friendly. Instead of asking whether the review should be public or private at the beginning of the process, I would move the question to the end:

1. Customer scans the QR code or clicks the link.

2. Customer selects the number of stars and writes their review text \(optionally supported by AI\).

3. Customer chooses whether the review should be public or private. Private is selected by default, regardless of whether the review is positive or negative.

At this point, a pop-up gating feature would be used:

If the review is positive \(configurable\), we would ask the customer again via a pop-up or additional message to make their review public. In this case, the star rating and text would be transferred to the selected platform and published there. If the review is negative, the additional notification would be omitted.

What do you think? I believe this wouldn't violate Google's guidelines.

replies.content: [@Lars Fuhrmann](obj://user/3f6f78e020bf4d6f8f0c2ccd247476cd) Private is selected by default is Gating

comments.content: Glad you want to be compliant, but there are other platforms that we can use the filtering for! And that could be nice way to soften the blow for businesses with older reviews that are being removed at the moment because employee mentions and so on.

[@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf)

replies.content:
[@Brian Carter](obj://user/b8245cb18c6f4f1ba6e91082b130255c) there are two parts in this feature:

comments.content:
I think the public/private feedback workflow approach is excellent.

For staff mentions, I think it's good to have an information for the client that it's not allowed by Google to ask for staff mention \(or any other content/mention\):
[ul][li]the feature can be only about mentions[li]the "multi-link/QR code" part is also very useful for scanning/usage tracking and statistics[li]it's still allowed to gently ask for mentioning staff members on other platforms

An other feature that is affected and was not mentioned in the post is the AI suggestion, for 2 reasons:
[ul][li]it guides the customer on what content its review should include so goes against not "request that specific content be included."[li]Google said it will use automatic systems to identify any review written by AI and delete it, even if it reflects a genuine experience.
It's still a great feature that can be useful on many platforms but:
[ul][li]there should be a warning that it cannot be used on Google[li]when an AI generated review is created through this feature, Google should be removed from the list of platforms it can be copy/pasted to.

Thanks for opening the discussion and keep going to always more compliance.

Ive been thinking about this for sometime now as I felt the flow would be considered again Googles TOS, so I think this is very wise and practical to have in place. I think its important how you ask for a review. I think asking for a customers experience and what brought them to the establishment would be ok to ask for their experience.

Thanks for your hard work. [@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) and the team.

Hi Giacomo, thanks for the update. Can you please you confirm a firm timeline? We need dates so we can notify clients in advance - some bought mainly for the filtering feature and will be unhappy \(a few may cancel\).

[@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) I would like to see Offer & Event placed higher on development more so now than before because of Google Policy change! Since the Flywheel System cater more to businesses that have physical customers like: Restaurants, Gyms, Dentist etc. Those service businesses reply on Offers and Events a lot to get and maintain customers, clients & patients. Also those two features from Google are great business sticking features!

[@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) The Google restriction on gating reviews is not new; I already pointed this out 1.5 years ago. The wording in the Google terms was identical to the current one – unfortunately, no one wanted to listen. However, Climbo can be connected to more than 20 review platforms, all of which have different guidelines and not all of them prohibit gating. Therefore, I do not understand the move – especially at this point in time. Rather, every agency should state in their terms and conditions that every customer must familiarize themselves with the terms and conditions of the review platform themselves, and it must be pointed out that some platforms, like Google, prohibit gating and that the account can be suspended.

An equally important point, however \(regardless of Google\), is the sending of thousands of spam emails through the account, which can lead to the suspension of the entire account. Something urgently needs to be done about this.

It would be nice if someone could also comment again on the first point.

replies.content: [@Hajo Rappe](obj://user/0a2fbb7a55274c5a929198d1a4f44d0c)Your first point is inaccurate because almost all major review platforms have identical anti gating policies. Google, Tripadvisor, Trustpilot, and Yelp all explicitly prohibit selectively soliciting positive reviews or discouraging negative feedback. Furthermore, regulatory bodies like the FTC outlaw review gating entirely. Because the practice is illegal under consumer protection laws, shifting liability to the client through agency terms and conditions does not protect the business from legal penalties or profile suspensions.

title: 🚀 This Week’s Development Focus
content:
Hello Climbers,

This week, we’re starting work on something big: the pre-built website for your clients.

We’re building a ready-to-use website structure that your clients can launch instantly. All they need to do is connect their Google Business profile, and the website is automatically generated using their real business data: description, business information, opening hours, photos, reviews, services, attributes, and social links. There’s no manual setup, no copy-pasting, and no technical work required.

But this isn’t just a simple auto-generated website. The entire structure is optimized for LLM discovery, meaning it’s designed to be easily understood, indexed, and surfaced by AI search engines. On top of that, our upcoming GEO Agent will continuously update the website with fresh, relevant content, automatically generating new updates based on incoming reviews. This ensures the site stays dynamic, further strengthening your client’s visibility and improving their ranking across AI-driven search results.

This isn’t just a website. It’s an AI-optimized visibility engine built for the future of local search.

These are not small updates, so development may take more than one week. But as always, on Thursday’s AMA, we’ll show you the progress live and walk you through what’s coming 👀

Alongside this, we’re:
• Fixing minor bugs
• Polishing small UX details
• Releasing a few minor features in the background

And don’t forget 👇

🔥 Today — Wayne’s Sales Accelerator
🕘 9 PM Rome time

If you want to improve conversions and close more deals, be there live for the Q&A.

Big week ahead.
Let’s build. 💪

title: 🚀 This Week’s Development Focus — ⚠️ Website Update!
content:
Hello Climbers,

Here’s what we’re working on this week.

1️⃣ Finishing the Website Feature

We’re finalizing the new website feature — and there’s an important change that comes directly from your feedback.

Instead of keeping the website as a sub-feature inside the Profile section, we’ve decided to make it one of the main platform features \(it will become the 6th core feature\). It will be activable/deactivable from your Agency custom plans.

This gives you full flexibility.

If you want to keep your offer highly vertical and focused \(for example, reviews-only\), you can simply deactivate the website feature from your custom plans.

If you want to include it as part of a higher-tier package, bundle it, or even sell it separately — you can do that too.

So if you're pitching a business that already has an established website, you can offer a plan that does not include the website feature.

If you're pitching a business that doesn’t have a website yet, you can include it in the plan, increase the perceived value, and charge accordingly.

This way, everyone wins — flexibility at the agency level.

2️⃣ Instant Google Reviews Import + Instant Notifications

We’ve figured out how to properly work with Google webhooks, and we’re building a system that will:

• Instantly import Google reviews as soon as they are left by end customers
• Trigger an email notification when a new review is imported
• Trigger a push notification for those who have the mobile app

This means no more delays — real-time visibility.

End customers leave a review → it appears immediately inside the platform → your clients get notified instantly.

This will massively improve perceived value and engagement.

We’ll keep pushing hard this week.
As always, thank you for the feedback — it’s shaping the product in real time.

Let’s build. 💪

title: 🚧 This Week Development Focus
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

Here’s what we’ll be building and refining this week.

🔄 Reorder review platforms on the review link

You’ll be able to choose the order of platforms \(Google, Facebook, Trustpilot, etc.\) shown to the end customer, so you can prioritize the platform that matters most for your client’s visibility.

🎥 Video reviews on widgets

If a customer leaves a video testimonial, it can now be displayed directly inside your review widgets, helping your clients build stronger trust and improve website conversion.

📊 Direct Google Sheets integration

Connect a spreadsheet and automatically import newly added contacts into the platform, no Zapier required.

🔗 Webhook Builder \(Agency Mode\)

Create webhook subscriptions for platform events directly inside Climbo and send them to any destination URL. Perfect for connecting with CRMs, automation tools, or custom backends.

🤖 Enable AI Chatbot

Activate an AI chatbot in Client Mode that automatically answers common customer questions.

✉️ Change client login email

You can finally update a client’s login email without recreating the account.

🌐 Website improvements

More control over the website feature, including the ability to safely disconnect a domain and manage settings more cleanly.

🎟 Coupon V2

Apply coupons to specific plans and even to existing customers without changing their subscription, making promotions, retention offers, and custom deals much easier to manage.

Thank you again, your feedback is literally shaping the product every week 🙌

As always, all new features will be presented during the next AMA on Thursday at 4:00 PM \(Rome time\).

title: Prospecting Tool
content:
Last week, I attended the Horecava every day, the largest hospitality industry event in the Netherlands. All major and minor horeca entrepreneurs and companies that sell products or services in this sector come together here. A few days beforehand, a list of all participating companies was made available. This was a great opportunity for me to review it using the prospecting tool.

For the companies where I saw potential opportunities, I printed out the reports and handed them over. Despite the entrepreneurs being very busy, they made time for me and appreciated that I had done my "homework." I increasingly noticed which approach resonated best with each entrepreneur. I’ve already received many commitments and a lot of interest.

This coming week, I will follow up on these leads and try to convert them into new clients. I also noticed that large companies tend to leave relatively few reviews for their business. During the event, topics such as trust, Google ranking, SEO, GEO, AI, LLM ranking, and others were discussed. At the end of the event, we toasted to celebrate the successful days.

The prospecting tool is an incredibly valuable resource! Hopefully, in the future, it will be even more beautifully designed, with more information and statistics included, but I have full confidence in that. To everyone in this new community, I would say: make good use of it!

A quick shoutout to the Climbo team. I’ve been with you for a while now, and you’ve taken the right direction with Climbo 2.0. Keep up the great work!

title: 🚀 Development Update — What we’re working on this week
content:
Hi Climbers,

Here’s a quick update on what the team is building right now. A lot of powerful things are coming that will help you sell more, retain clients better, and show value more clearly.

🔧 New Features in Development

AI Activity
A new section where your clients can see everything the AI agents are doing automatically \(replies, posts, actions, etc.\).
Perfect to show the real work happening in the background and reinforce the value of your service.

Free Trial Banner
Trial accounts will see a top banner with custom text + button \(for example:
“You are on free trial. To upgrade, add a payment method. Click here.”\)
This will help increase trial → paid conversions.

Custom AI Prompt
You’ll be able to add custom instructions to AI agents \(tone of voice, style, brand personality\) so replies truly match your client’s voice.

Video on Review Link
Upload a short welcome video on the first page of the review link \(business owner greeting + asking for a review\).
This can significantly increase review conversion.

Connect Google during Onboarding \(skippable\)
During client onboarding, after adding the first location, they can connect their Google Business Profile immediately.
This means they enter the platform with all sections already filled.
Still optional — they can skip and connect later.

Reply to all past Google reviews
One-click bulk reply to old Google reviews \(with safe rate limits so Google doesn’t flag automation\).
Amazing for SEO and Google rankings.

🎓 New Tutorials & Resources

Automations Course
A brand new course on how to properly set up automations for your clients.

Mobile App Application Course
Step-by-step training on how to complete the mobile app application, create screenshots, and write promotional texts.

New Unbranded Client Tutorials + Chatbot
We’re filling the unbranded knowledge base here:
👉 [https://knowledge-base.help](https://knowledge-base.help)

It will include:

new unbranded tutorial videos

a chatbot trained on the docs

You can simply send your clients there for support, or embed the chatbot directly in your platform.

☁️ Quick note about yesterday’s Cloudflare issue

Yesterday we had a spike of requests that temporarily exceeded our Cloudflare plan limit.
This caused a few hours of instability.

We’ve now upgraded to an unlimited requests plan, so this cannot happen again.

The platform is now fully operational — sorry for the inconvenience and thank you for your patience.

More details and live demos coming in Thursday’s AMA 👀
Make sure to add the event from the Calendar.

Let’s keep building 🚀

Roadmap Link: [https://roadmap.climbo.com/](https://roadmap.climbo.com/)

title: 🤩 Employee Tracking, GEO Agent, New UI & What’s Next – A.M.A. Recap (23/04/2026)
content:
Hi Climbers!

Here’s a quick recap of yesterday’s AMA and what we covered.

We started with some clarifications on the GEO Agent. We went deeper into how the system will generate localized, SEO-focused content using real business data and reviews, and how you can position this feature in your offer. This is just the first version, and we’ll keep refining it based on your feedback.

We also presented the new Client Mode UI, where all features are now grouped into three main areas: Reputation, Visibility, and Engagement. This is a UI improvement designed to make the platform much clearer and easier to use for local business owners, without changing any existing functionality.

A big part of the AMA was dedicated to the employee tracking & leaderboard feature, which is now ready and will be live today in a few hours.

With this feature, businesses can add their employees \(with name, photo, and aliases\) and generate a dedicated review link and QR code for each one. Every link tracks unique visits, allowing the business to see which employee is actively asking for reviews.

We’ve also added the ability to assign reviews to employees when their alias is mentioned inside the review text. This means employees are not only motivated to generate visits, but also to build real relationships with customers and encourage them to leave more personal, authentic reviews.

On top of that, we’re introducing a public leaderboard \(last 7 days, 30 days, all-time\) where businesses can rank employees based on performance. This creates a natural incentive system, where employees are encouraged to ask more customers for reviews.

The goal is to create a simple but powerful gamification system that businesses can use to reward top performers and increase review generation across the team.

Looking ahead, we shared what’s coming next on the Agency Mode side, including improvements like moving clients from free to paid plans more smoothly and introducing sales team management features.

Finally, we mentioned some ongoing updates, including work on Apple Business and Google Sheets integration.

Thanks again to everyone who joined live and contributed with questions and feedback. Keep sharing your thoughts in the community, they’re a key part of how we build the product.

Climbo Team 🫡

So basically we have to get used to it and educate our clients that the "update was made to provide a clearer, more modern, and more user-friendly experience. It may need some time to get used to, but this design is intended to be better in the long run"

[@Ryan Nesbitt](obj://user/48dd3216010449a49c86bc19096d3f85) Totally understand the concern. Any UI change can feel uncomfortable at the beginning, especially for clients who were already used to the previous menu.
The reason behind the change is that the UI has to reflect the direction the platform is taking. We are constantly evolving and releasing new features every month, and we can’t keep scaling the client menu as one long list of features. It would become harder to understand over time.
That’s why we decided to group everything into the 3 main areas where the platform creates value for the client: Reputation, Visibility, and Engagement.
In the short term, some clients may need a little time to adjust. But if you look at the bigger picture, this structure helps them better understand why they are using the platform and what benefits they are getting from it — not just which feature they are clicking.

title: Bulk NFC Enabled Business Flyer idea 💡
content: Is there a bulk way to encode NFC Stickers, and does anyone know if they still work if passed through a Laminator? As I'm thinking of an out of hours leaflet drop to businesses and I currently have very little marketing budget so I was considering an NFC Introduction Business Flyer that's printed, NFC labelled, Laminated and then encoded with a business specific intro video/audio. What does everyone think?

comments.content:
[@Jason Leigh](obj://user/21fe80f0ebee4b6b9b3f240fd36abc0c), Last year \(January 2025\) I visited the Horecava in Amsterdam \(see a prospect image in the attachment\). In the weeks before the event I programmed about 600 NFC \(business\)cards with the GBP of the companies presenting at the event. For 4 days I ran my ass off at the event but made a great impression by just asking for the one responsible for marketing/getting more reviews because I got a gift for them. On an event, businesses pay a lot of money to get as much leads as possible so they don't have time for people trying to sell them something. But I got a gift for them that made them curious so most of them made time for me.

Most of them got a twinkle in their eyes when they saw how the NFC card showed the Google review page of their company when tapping their phone on it, because they never saw it before AND they could keep the NFC card. Time was to short so maybe I only could show about 250 NFC cards to attendees but on the other hand, I had a lot of prospect talks that I could normally never have in 4 days.

To be honest it was to much to follow up due to other existing clients etc. but the preliminary work and 4 days on the event helped me score 7 new clients for my online marketing services and the best made me 30K in the rest of 2025 and will be making me about 50K in 2026.

Why I'm saying this?

I mentioned earlier \(in other posts\) that nowadays our expansion span is no more than 5 seconds, so you need to make a great impression directly on the first handshake! In my experience a folder will be trown into the dustbin for 90% of the time but if you program the NFC card before you step into the store you have something very valuable to share and they will tell you all their "visibility problems". True story!

I'm not going to give everything away but look for a good supplier on Alibaba NOT Aliexpress and buy a couple hundred NFC cards for less than $0,38 a piece. A blank NFC tag is nearly as cheap/expensive as a fully branded NFC card The supplier can make a design for you or you can do it yourself in Canva. You have to go that extra mile and you will having something unique. \(I like my own branding on my NFC cards. Small but visible\).

For the one who is interested, I can share in another post how you can directly find every businesses unique Google reviewlink. I already shared a document on how to program a NFC product but will share it in the attachment as well.

Good luck!

title: Our first paying client
content:
I just wanted to share a story for those of you who are new...... I've never posted here before.

We signed up in May of 2025 and we're dragging our feet out of fear. I finally decided to give it a shot and approached a Thai restaurant for a free trial. Because English is NOT their first language, They were very excited about the AI replies. Then my husband and I went to Florida on our 20-year anniversary and I pitched a French bakery in fort Lauderdale. When we got home we on boarded our third free trial a hibachi and sushi place nearby. My strategy has been to pitch businesses who do not have English as their first language as they are very excited about the AI replies!! These were the first three business I ever pitched and I was shocked to hear that they all said yes.

We offered them all a one month trial. Happy to report that we have our FIRST PAYING client the Thai restaurant is purchasing it for both of his locations!! We are charging 200 a month for that one. I just found out today that the French bakery wants to continue and is excited about it That's a 100 per month. Hibachi restaurant loves it too but we are doing a trade basically our family of six gets to eat there for free anytime we want.

I want to thank my mentor!!! You are amazing You know who you are 🥰

Anyone out there who is new believe that you can do this and just start with the free trials. We don't even know how to do anything other than the QR codes And we are making money.

comments.content:
[@Anne Kibler](obj://user/db20336ac5e34af38be9b6e99df1829a) This is an amazing story, congratulations on your first paying clients! 🎉

What I love most here is how simple and real your approach is:
• local businesses
• face to face
• free trials
• solving a very clear pain point \(language + replies\)

Your strategy with non-native English businesses is brilliant. AI replies are huge value there, and you explained it in a way they could immediately understand.

And this line is gold:

“We only know how to use QR codes and we are making money.”

That’s the perfect lesson for everyone here 👆
You don’t need to master the whole platform.
You just need to help one business solve one real problem.

$200/month + $100/month + free hibachi for life 😄 that’s how MRR starts.

So proud of you. Keep going, and please share more wins like this.
You’re proving that this really works 🚀

title: It took some time but then 💥BOOOM💥
content:
Cheerio Climbers,
I started dealing with Climbo 2,5 months ago. I set up my website and watched most of the tutorials. Super hyped I contacted businesses in my surroundings \(I focus on restaurants and cafés\). To be honest: It was draining. Most restaurant owner were not very pleased to listen to my offer - after they've been contacted by Google and other local business directories before. I really started to doubt myself and whether I should change my niche. But I kept going.
Since this week the penny dropped and I've closed 5 restaurants on an annual plan. 3.000 € in one week is pretty awesome and really excites me. 😍
Interestingly once a local business starts using the software, their competitors also feel the urge to sign up. Good for me, good for them, good for us.

Good luck to all of you who push through initial challenges as well. Keep going 🙌

title: GDPR Compliant
content:
Could somebody inform me; is Climbo 2.0 fully GDPR compliant?
I know there where some issues in the past, just wondering if this has all been solved with the 2.0 version. Thanks :\)

comments.content: In this video, I want to clarify our platform's compliance with GDPR regulations. I assure you that we are fully compliant, as we always include an opt-out link in our messages, allowing end customers to unsubscribe easily. Additionally, we require consent from clients before sending messages, and our opt-in page ensures that customer details are collected with proper consent. It's crucial that end customers also consent to the processing of their personal data when leaving feedback.

replies.content: [@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) Please embed Google fonts locally, otherwise the platform is not GDPR compliant. 🙏🏼

I was just looking through the developer tools and noticed some external URLs for the google fonts. Are these currently being loaded directly from Google’s servers? I’m asking because, from a GDPR perspective, this is problematic due to the transfer of the visitor's IP address.

If they are indeed loaded externally, would it be possible to host them locally on our own server instead?

Also in the text besides the Review Link Checkboxes really should be the possibility to add the link to the privacy policy of the client. We should also give business owners the option to add their own links directly from the “Other” section in the Review Link settings, so the page shows the business’s links instead of the agency’s.

title: Fortune Favours The Brave
content:
Sorry for the long post
So every few weeks for at least the last 7 years i have gone to the same Pizza place, always order the same deal, always chatty with the guys there and before Christmas i was in there and i introduced myself to the owner \(he is very rarely there\) and we had a good chat nothing business just friendly conversation.

Now this evening with the help of [@Gurby Bulo](obj://user/5527cd16ad8945c790ed594e3e72cb25) post earlier i was able to set up this NFC & QR Code stand before going in \(at this point i do not have an active climbo account yet\) so the boss just happened to be in there this even, i said hello happy new year..

Made my order and had a 2 min chat about the weather \(typical Brit am i right [@Andy Larder](obj://user/01bba9ce9b6943ebae6972c0ef9dfb48) \) then i pulled out the stand from my back pocket and said i have a gift for you and presented the stand.
He wasn't 100% sure what it was, he read it he scanned it and his face lit up, very thankful and appreciative, he then asked immediately if i knew how to remove bad reviews, I explained that this is not possible but that with this and my help we can implement something that will and can improve his ranking and stars.

How much will this cost? I was gobsmacked, i grabbed a menu and asked him how much this advertising costs him \(50,000 printed and delivered nearly £1k\) I was amazed, at what he said next...

We are about to order a new print run of 50,000 soon......

I said that's amazing if we can get this QR code from the stand on the new leaflet run you will be in a great place to repair the reputation of the Google Business Profile...
I have got his contact number , and i will pop in next week when my Climbo is set up and get him onboarded...
Oh and he has another store 5 miles away and his son owns a Pharmacy in the same town...

WOW!! 2026 is going to be life changing thank you [@Tommaso Chinellato](obj://user/27c01345df6742a69b73afc23a363774) [@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) and [@Wayne Connell](obj://user/1ac82be8f98c4b9ab3de0adf4a76eaec) for the spark...

comments.content: Great job. You could’ve even said, “Heck, let me get those 50,000 menus printed for you too.” There are plenty of print vendors looking for orders, and that’s a solid volume. Easy extra income, and I’m sure he already has the PDF ready just place the order and ship it to him. Really great work all around.

[@Jason Leigh](obj://user/21fe80f0ebee4b6b9b3f240fd36abc0c) The fact he immediately asked about bad reviews and then about cost tells you everything. The pain is there. The awareness is there. The timing is perfect.

You’ve just turned a casual pizza routine into a multi-location opportunity… plus a pharmacy connection. That’s how this game works. Real local partnerships.

Proud of you for stepping up and making the move. 2026 doesn’t change lives. Decisions do 🚀
I forgot to say we've discussed putting the QR Code on the new Menu's !!!
